stimulus:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

  • Augusta Savage (1892–1962) was an African American sculptor and educator.
  • The Harp (1939) is a plaster sculpture by Savage.
  • It depicts a row of singing African American figures forming the strings of a harp.
  • It was commissioned for the 1939 New York World’s Fair.
  • The sculpture took its inspiration from the song “Lift Every Voice and Sing,” written by James Weldon Johnson in 1900.
  • Art historian Jeffreen Hayes describes the sculpture as “a monument to Black creative resilience.”
stem: The student wants to explain the sculpture’s specific historical context. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

answerOptions.content: According to art historian Jeffreen Hayes, The Harp, which depicts singing African American figures, is “a monument to Black creative resilience.”
The Harp (1939) is a plaster sculpture by Augusta Savage, an African American sculptor and educator who lived from 1892 to 1962.
Commissioned for the 1939 New York World’s Fair, Savage’s sculpture The Harp drew its inspiration from James Weldon Johnson’s 1900 song “Lift Every Voice and Sing.”
The Harp depicts a row of singing African American figures forming the strings of a harp.

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer. It situates the sculpture in its specific historical context: it was commissioned for the 1939 World’s Fair and was inspired by a 1900 song by James Weldon Johnson.

Choice A quotes a critic’s general interpretation. Choice B gives biographical information about Savage. Choice D describes what the sculpture depicts. None of these explains the sculpture’s specific historical context.
